parentQuestion: Which of the following is the mildest consequence of traumatic brain injury?

options: Brain damage caused by external force, ranging from mild to severe
scaffolds.explain: TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY (TBI) is brain damage from EXTERNAL FORCE (impact, acceleration). It exists on a spectrum from mild (concussion) to severe.

wrongExplains.0: TBI is caused by trauma, not genetics.
wrongExplains.2: Stroke is vascular; TBI is traumatic/mechanical injury.
wrongExplains.3: Tumors are abnormal growths; TBI is traumatic injury.

options: The mildest form of TBI — temporary dysfunction without visible structural damage
scaffolds.explain: A CONCUSSION is the MILDEST form of TBI — TEMPORARY disruption of brain function without structural damage visible on imaging.

wrongExplains.0: Concussions typically resolve; they're not permanent damage.
wrongExplains.2: Concussions are traumatic injuries, not tumors.
wrongExplains.3: Concussions can occur without skull fracture; they're functional disruption.

scaffolds.q: Why don't concussions show on CT or MRI scans?
options: Because scanners don't work on the brain
Because there's no visible structural damage — it's functional disruption
Because they're too severe to image
scaffolds.explain: Concussions don't show on imaging because there's NO VISIBLE STRUCTURAL DAMAGE — the brain doesn't work right temporarily, but there's no tearing or bleeding.

wrongExplains.0: Scanners do work; concussions just don't cause visible damage.
wrongExplains.2: Concussions affect brain function, not bones.
wrongExplains.3: Concussions are mild; more severe injuries show on imaging.

scaffolds.q: How long do concussion symptoms typically last?
options: Days to weeks — most resolve completely
scaffolds.explain: Most concussions RESOLVE COMPLETELY within DAYS TO WEEKS. Headache, confusion, and dizziness clear up as the brain recovers.

wrongExplains.0: Most concussions resolve; permanent effects are unusual.
wrongExplains.2: Symptoms last longer than seconds, typically days to weeks.
wrongExplains.3: Most resolve in weeks; prolonged symptoms are the exception.

options: Brain swelling — more serious than concussion
scaffolds.explain: CEREBRAL EDEMA is BRAIN SWELLING. In a rigid skull, swelling increases pressure, compresses blood vessels, and can be LIFE-THREATENING. Much worse than concussion.

wrongExplains.0: Cerebral edema is serious, not mild.
wrongExplains.2: Edema is pathological swelling, not just a headache.
wrongExplains.3: Edema is abnormal swelling, not normal function.

options: Bleeding in or around the brain — more serious than concussion
scaffolds.explain: Brain HEMORRHAGE means BLEEDING — epidural, subdural, or intracerebral. These often require emergency neurosurgery. Far worse than concussion.

wrongExplains.2: Hemorrhage is serious bleeding, not just a headache.
wrongExplains.3: Hemorrhage is pathological bleeding, not normal.

scaffolds.q: What is 'confusion' in the context of TBI?
options: A symptom that can accompany various TBI severities
scaffolds.explain: CONFUSION is a SYMPTOM, not a diagnosis. It can accompany various TBI severities — concussions cause confusion, but so can more severe injuries.

wrongExplains.0: Confusion is a symptom, not a TBI category.
wrongExplains.2: Confusion is a symptom seen across severities, not a severity level.
wrongExplains.3: Confusion is a symptom, not a treatment.

scaffolds.q: Rank these from mildest to most severe: Hemorrhage, concussion, edema.
options: Concussion → edema → hemorrhage (all more severe than concussion)
scaffolds.explain: CONCUSSION is mildest (temporary dysfunction, no structural damage). EDEMA and HEMORRHAGE involve visible tissue damage and are much more severe.

wrongExplains.3: Concussion is clearly milder than bleeding or swelling.

scaffolds.q: Which is the mildest form of traumatic brain injury?
scaffolds.explain: CONCUSSION is the MILDEST TBI — temporary dysfunction, no structural damage, usually full recovery. Everything else involves visible tissue damage.

wrongExplains.0: Edema (swelling) involves structural changes; concussion doesn't.
wrongExplains.2: Hemorrhage (bleeding) is far more serious than concussion.
wrongExplains.3: Contusion (bruising) involves structural damage; concussion doesn't.
